coredata.dc:creator: Shekaari, Ashkan
Abolhassani, Mohammad Reza
coredata.dc:description:
First-principles molecular dynamics has been applied to inquire into the melting behaviors of n-atom (
n = 6 , 10
) graphene quantum dots (GQD6 and zigzag GQD10) within the temperature range of T =0–500K. The temperature dependence of the geometry of each quantum dot is thoroughly evaluated via calculating the related shape deformation parameters and the eigenvalues of the quadrupole tensors. Examining the variations of some phase-transition indicators such as root-mean-square bond length fluctuations and mean square displacements broadly proposes the value of
T
m
= 70
K for the melting point of GQD6 while a continuous, two-stage phase transition has been concluded for zigzag GQD10.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Density-functional theory
Molecular dynamics
Graphene quantum dot

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematics of hypothetical transistors based on (a) GQD6 and (b) zigzag GQD10, tunnel-coupled to source and drain–visualized by XCrySDen [31].

Fast-fluctuating patterns of the instantaneous temperature of GQD6 around the target ones
T = 100
, 200, and 300K with the averages of about 99.83, 199.62, and 299.39K, respectively, indicating how temperature–as a macroscopic quality–arises microscopically from quantum–mechanical particle fluctuations. As is seen, the amplitude of the fluctuations becomes relatively larger as the temperature rises.
The temperature dependence of the geometry of GQD6 from zero to 500K. Each subfigure contains both the front view and the side view of the related geometry.
The evolution of the geometry of zigzag GQD10 from zero to 350K.
The shape deformation parameter as a function of temperature for GQD6 and zigzag GQD10.
The temperature dependence of the eigenvalues of the quadrupole tensor for (a) GQD6 and (b) zigzag GQD10.

The
δ
rms
as a function of temperature.
The MSD of (a) GQD6, and (b) zigzag GQD10 averaged over the constituent atoms as a movie during the last 3.7ps.

The temperature dependence of the average values of (a)
E
tot
, (b)
E
kin
ion
, (c)
E
kin
fic
, (d)
E
XC
, (e)
E
es
, (f)
E
pp
loc
, (g)
E
pp
nl
, and (h)
E
pot
calculated for GQD6.

The temperature dependence of the average values of (a)
E
tot
, (b)
E

, (d)
E
XC
, (e)

E

, and (h)

calculated for zigzag GQD10.

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Plasma Physics Research Center, Science and Research Branch, Islamic Azad University, 14778-93855 Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
First-principles molecular dynamics has been applied to inquire into the melting behaviors of n-atom (
n = 6 , 10
) graphene quantum dots (GQD6 and zigzag GQD10) within the temperature range of T =0–500K. The temperature dependence of the geometry of each quantum dot is thoroughly evaluated via calculating the related shape deformation parameters and the eigenvalues of the quadrupole tensors. Examining the variations of some phase-transition indicators such as root-mean-square bond length fluctuations and mean square displacements broadly proposes the value of
T
m
= 70
K for the melting point of GQD6 while a continuous, two-stage phase transition has been concluded for zigzag GQD10.

ce:section.ce:para:
Over the past decade, much studies have been focused on different, intriguing properties of two-dimensional nanomaterials [1–8], particularly on graphene because of spread spectrum of its applications in many diverse areas such as nanoelectronics, optoelectronics, bioelectronics, etc. [9], leading to rapidly-accumulating literature in the realm of low-dimensional systems as well. It has demonstrated excellent electronic, mechanical and optical characteristics including ultrahigh mobility, outstanding flexibility and stability, excellent thermal conductivity (5000Wm−1 K−1), high transmittance within the visible infrared region, exceptional room-temperature electron mobility (2.5×105 cm2 V−1 s−1), large specific surface area (2630m2 g−1), a Young’s modulus of about 1 TPa and intrinsic strength of 130 GPa [10–15]. Enjoying outstanding electrical conductivity, optical transparency, mechanical flexibility, and two-dimensionality has made it a promising candidate for transparent and conductive electrodes [16]. Unlike conventional metal electrodes, graphene has the advantage that its Fermi level, and therefore, its workfunction could be tailored by chemical doping or electrostatic gating [17,18], making it to be of great use in device applications such as high-efficiency chemically-doped solar cells and gate-controlled variable Schottky barrier devices [19,20]. It has also an optical absorption of about
π α ≈ 2.3 %
in the infrared limit (with
α
the fine-structure constant), complete impermeability to any gases, and the ability to sustain extremely high densities of electric current (a million times larger than copper) [15]. It is a basic building block for graphitic materials of all dimensionalities, being wrapped into zero-dimensional fullerene or quantum dots, rolled into one-dimensional nanotubes, or stacked into three-dimensional graphite.

The technological drive for making electronic devices continuously smaller has some interesting consequences: it is now routinely possible to make small electron boxes in solid-state devices, containing an integer number of conduction electrons [21]. Such devices are usually operated as transistors via field-effect gates, called single-electron transistors. In semiconductor boxes, the number of trapped electrons could be reduced to zero, one, two, etc. Such semiconductor single-electron transistors are called quantum dots. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional graphene nanoparticles with confined electrons in all the three spatial dimensions leading to quantization of their spectrum. They have excellent optical properties and biosecurity, exhibiting remarkable prospects in biomedical fields such as cell imaging and biosensors [22], also leading to state-of-the-art display technologies such as quantum dot light-emitting devices (QD-LED) [23].

The density-functional molecular dynamics (DFMD) method introduced by Car and Parrinello has been applied to investigate the finite-temperature behaviors of GQD6 and zigzag GQD10 via adopting a self-consistent plane-wave pseudopotential approach [32] as implemented in Quantum ESPRESSO integrated suite [33]. The generalized gradient approximation [34] proposed by Perdew, Burke and Ernzerhof (PBE) [35] has been applied for the exchange and correlation functionals. Effects of the atomic core (non-valence electrons) have been described using a scalar-relativistic ultrasoft pseudopotential [36,37] generated by Rappe-Rabe-Kaxiras-Joannopoulos (RRKJ) pseudization method [38] with nonlinear core correction [39], while the valence shell is considered to be
2 s 2 p
. The electron density is augmented through a Fourier interpolation scheme in real space [40]. For GQD6 and zigzag GQD10, respectively, the kinetic energy cut-offs of about 60 and 65 Rydberg (Ryd) for the wave functions, and 250 and 260Ryd for the charge densities and the potentials have been found to be enough to reach energy convergence. Free cubic cells containing six atoms in a honeycomb array (Fig. 1 (a)), and ten atoms in a graphene-like zigzag array (Fig. 1(b)) have been used under periodic boundary conditions with vacuum spaces of about 15Å along the three spatial dimensions to decouple the weakest possible periodic interactions. Fixed-cell relaxations based on the Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (BFGS) optimization method [41–43] have been applied on the atomic positions to find the relaxed ones in a way that the magnitude of each component of the total force on each atom drops to less than 7.35×10−5 Ryd/Å. The forces in a Car–Parrinello molecular dynamics (CPMD) calculation are the partial derivative of the Kohn–Sham energy (
E
KS
) with respect to the nuclear positions (
R
I
) and the Kohn–Sham orbitals (
Φ
i
). The orbital forces are calculated as the action of the Kohn–Sham Hamiltonian (
H
KS
) on the orbitals:
F
Φ
i
= -
f
i
H
KS
ϕ
i
= -
δ
E
KS
δ
ϕ
i
∗
with
{
f
i
}
integer occupation numbers, whereas the forces associated to the nuclear degrees of freedom could be expressed as
F
R
I
= -
∂
E
KS
∂
R
I

The corresponding Newtonian equations of motion for both the orbitals and nuclear positions could be obtained by the Car–Parrinello (CP) Lagrangian
L
CP
R
N
,
R
̇
N
,
Φ
i
,
Φ
̇
i
=
1
2
∑
I
M
I
R
̇
I
2
+
1
2
∑
i
μ 〈
Φ
̇
i
Φ
̇
i
〉 -
∊
KS
Φ
i
,
R
N
with
∊
KS
the extended Kohn–Sham energy functional, and
M
I
the ionic mass [44]. The fictitious electron mass (the inertia parameter assigned to the orbital degrees of freedom,
μ
) in the CP Lagrangian, used to control the time evolution of the electronic degrees of freedom, is chosen to be 200a.u. (200 times the mass of an electron) with mass cut-off of about 2.5Ryd for the Fourier acceleration effective mass in order to prevent the quality of the simulations to be affected adversely and having minimized the electron drag effect. The nuclei evolve in time at a certain, instantaneous physical temperature
∝
∑
I
M
I
R
̇
I
2
. Although temperature is a collective effect and is mainly intelligible in dealing with macroscopic systems including large numbers of particles of the order of the Avogadro’s number, the kinetic theory [45] providing a microscopic explanation of temperature based on the movements of the constituent particles enables DFMD simulations to apply temperature to few-particle systems through the equipartition theorem [46],
T =
2 〈 E 〉
3
Nk
B
with N the number of constituent atoms and
k
B
the Boltzmann constant. In the first CPMD simulations, respectively for GQD6 and zigzag GQD10, extended electronic minimizations of 480 and 725 femtoseconds (fs)–with time steps of about 0.19 and 1.45fs to integrate the electronic and nuclear equations of motions–with fixed atoms and fixed cells are carried out in order to bring the electronic systems (electronic wave functions) on their ground states relative to the starting atomic configurations until the convergence of the kinetic energies associated to the fictitious electronic dynamics to a value less than
1 ×
10
- 7
Ryd, and the convergence of the total energies to those obtained from self-consistent-field calculations (−70.831 and −118.530Ryd) are achieved. After having minimized the electrons, the canonical (NVT) CPMD simulations restarting with zero initial velocities are performed on nuclear degrees of freedom at different temperatures using the standard Verlet algorithm [47,48] within 3630fs of the simulation time and with 2.41fs the time step. The electronic equations of motion are accelerated through a preconditioning scheme [49]. The temperature of the systems changes from zero to 500K, controlled via rescaling the nuclear (ionic) velocities (no electronic temperature is applied to the simulations), leading to fluctuating patterns for the instantaneous temperature (Fig. 2 ) in a way that the average temperature each system experiences during each run is nearly equal to the target one with a so tiny difference of less than 1K.

The finite-temperature behaviors of the quantum dots have been investigated via calculating a number of thermodynamic phase-transition indicators such as root-mean-square bond length fluctuations (
δ
rms
) [50] and mean square displacements (MSD) at different points within the aforementioned temperature range. The
δ
rms
is a measure of fluctuations in the bond lengths averaged over all the constituent carbon atoms and over the total time span, being defined as
δ
rms
=
2
N ( N - 1 )
∑
i > j
(
〈
r
ij
2
〉
t
- 〈
r
ij
〉
t
2
)
1 / 2
〈
r
ij
〉
t
,
with
r
ij
the distance between atoms i and j, and
〈 …
〉
t
the time average over the entire trajectory. The average MSD is another traditional parameter to determine the phase transition (solid-like to liquid-like) of the quantum dots, being expressed as
〈
r
2
t
〉 =
1
N
∑
n = 1
N
R
n
t
-
R
n
t
0
2
with N the number of particles to be averaged,
R
n
t
0
the reference position of the
n th
atom, and
R
n
t
the position of the
n th
atom in the determined time t. The finite-temperature evolutions of the geometries of the quantum dots are also evaluated via calculating the related shape deformation parameters, defined as
∊
def
=
2
Q
x
Q
y
+
Q
z
where
Q
x
⩾
Q
y
⩾
Q
z
are the eigenvalues, in descending order, of the quadrupole tensor
Q
ij
=
∑
I
R
Ii
R
Ij
in a way that i and j run from 1 to 3, I runs over the number of atoms, and
R
Ii
is the
i th
coordinate of the
I th
atom with respect to the center of mass of the system. The value of
∊
def
for the reference structure of GQD6 at absolute zero is computed 2 with
Q
x
=
Q
y
= 1.35
and
Q
z
= 0
, indicating its complete hexagonal geometry (regular hexagon with dihedral D6 symmetry group) and two-dimensionality in the
x - y
plane. Any larger or smaller values of
∊
def
implies deviation of the shapes of GQD6 or zigzag GQD10 from regular hexagonality or decahedrality, respectively.

The evolutions of the geometries of GQD6 and zigzag GQD10 with temperature are illustrated in Figs. 3 and 4
, respectively.

The shape deformation parameter
∊
def
and the eigenvalues of the quadrupole tensor as functions of temperature are also plotted in Figs. 5 and 6
respectively, showing in more detail the temperature dependence of the shapes of GQD6 and zigzag GQD10.

It is seen that for GQD6, the values of
∊
def
are mostly close to 2 (regular hexagon), indicating that the quantum dot, though not completely, preserves its hexagonality during the melting process. For zigzag GQD10, the values satisfying
∊
def
> 2.5
are related to elliptical shapes (zero to 150K) while
∊
def
< 2
refers to irregular geometries equally extended in two perpendicular directions (200–500K). As seen in Fig. 4, up to 80K, and at 150K, there exist atomic structures of high reflection symmetry (mirror symmetry). It could be so illustrative to examine the three eigenvalues of the quadrupole tensors (
Q
x
,
Q
y
,
Q
z
). From Fig. 6(a), it is seen that two of the eigenvalues are always considerably larger than those at absolute zero (
Q
x
=
Q
y
= 1.35
), indicating that the distances of the atoms from the center of mass significantly increase as temperature rises. This is a clear manifestation of that the geometry of the quantum dot is beginning to change. Up to 60K, the first two eigenvalues are nearly equal while the third one is much smaller (
Q
z
≪
Q
x
≈
Q
y
), exhibiting that the evolution of the geometry of GQD6 with temperature dominantly takes place in the
x - y
plane: the system begins to melt in two dimensions. At
T =
70K–which will be shown to be the melting point of the quantum dot–the constituent atoms are mainly located in the
y - z
plane (
Q
y
≈
Q
z
≫
Q
x
), and then at 80K, the atoms are again located in the
x - y
plane. As a consequance, at
T ⩾
70K, GQD6 melts in three dimensions. From Fig. 6(b), it is seen that at absolute zero,
Q
x
>
Q
y
, and
Q
z
= 0
, and so the atomic structure of zigzag GQD10 is mainly extended in x direction. For
0 < T ≤ 80
K, the quantum dot is mainly located in the
x - y
plane with a larger extension in the y direction due to
Q
y
>
Q
x
≫
Q
z
. Such a trend is violated at 100K, and established at 150K again. As seen in Fig. 4, it is interesting to note that up to 80K, and for 150K, there could be seen high-symmetric atomic structures. For
T > 150
K, the change in the atomic structure takes place in three dimensions. Similar to GQD6, two of the eigenvalues are always significantly larger than those at absolute zero (
Q
x
= 24.16
and
Q
y
= 8.83
), indicating increase in the distances of the atoms from the center of mass with rise in temperature.

Fig. 7 illustrates the
δ
rms
of the quantum dots as functions of temperature. As it is seen, there exists a sharp, abrupt rise from 70 to 100K for GQD6, a slower rise from 100 to 400K, and finally a sharp increase from 400 to 500K.

Moreover, from Fig. 8 (a), it could be seen that up to 60K, the MSD of GQD6 remains within a small range of less than 0.23Å2 while at 70K, it rises sharply and reaches to a value more than 3Å2. The coexistence of such a major difference in the MSD from 60 to 70K and the abrupt change observed in the
δ
rms
at about 70K is a clear manifestation of phase transition, and strongly proposes the value of
T
m
=
70K for the melting point of the quantum dot at which the atomic arrangement of the system undergoes major changes due to weakening of interatomic forces, leading to broken CC bonds as well.

For zigzag GQD10, on the other hand, no abrupt change could be seen in the
δ
rms
(Fig. 7), and there exist gradual rises from 50 to 300K, and from 350 to 500K, indicating a continuous, two-stage phase transition without any definite value as the melting point. It could be so illustrative examining the related MSDs. As seen in Fig. 8(b), from 300 to 350K, the MSD decreases and this exactly corresponds with the decrease in the related
δ
rms
(Fig. 7) from its local maximum at 300 to 350K. From 350 to 500K, the MSD has an increasing trend in accord with the changes in the related root-mean-square amplitude of atomic vibrations. Up to 80K, where nearly-ordered structures with mirror symmetry emerge (Fig. 4), the MSD makes oscillating patterns with decreasing amplitudes within a small range of less than 1.5Å2. It could be seen that whenever the MSD of zigzag GQD10 decreases in passing from
T
1
to
T
2
>
T
1
, a transition from disordered to nearly-ordered, mirror-symmetric atomic structure takes place: from 100 to 150K, the MSD decreases and therefore, at 150K, the atomic arrangement becomes ordered. The same holds in passing from 300 to 350K.

Figs. 9 and 10
illustrate the average values of the total energy (
E
tot
) and its different constituent contributions including the kinetic energy of the nuclear dynamics (
E
kin
ion
), the fictitious kinetic energy of the electron dynamics (
E
kin
fic
), the exchange–correlation contribution (
E
XC
), the electrostatic energy (
E
es
), the potential energy (
E
pot
), and the local (
E
pp
loc
) and the non-local (
E
pp
nl
) components of the pseudopotential energy as functions of temperature for GQD6 and zigzag GQD10, respectively. The total energy of each system could be simply calculated as the summation of the preceding individual terms.

From Figs. 9(a) and 10(a), as expected, it is seen that the total energies have ever-increasing trends, indicating that the atomic structures of the quantum dots undergo less stability as temperature rises. Figs. 9(b) and 10(b) show that the values of
E
kin
ion
make no specific patterns and fluctuate around 42 and 70.4Ryd for GQD6 and zigzag GQD10, respectively. For
E
kin
fic
there exist ever-increasing trends as seen in Figs. 9(c) and 10(c), implying that rise in temperature directly affects the kinetic energy associated with the fictitious electron dynamics. Fig. 9(d) illustrates the increasing trend of
E
XC
for GQD6 with two local minima at 100 and 350K while that of zigzag GQD10 has an ever-increasing trend as seen in Fig. 10(d), indicating that the exchange–correlation contributions to stabilizing the atomic structures of the quantum dots decrease as temperature rises. As seen in Figs. 9(e) and 10(e), the
E
es
for GQD6 makes a fluctuating pattern, but that of zigzag GQD10 decreases up to 300K, and then increases. From Figs. 9(f), (g), 10(f) and (g), it is seen that the
E
pp
loc
for the quantum dots have increasing trends, while the
E
pp
nl
have decreasing trends both with fluctuating patterns. Interestingly, similar trends of those of the
E
pp
loc
also hold for the potential energies of the quantum dots as are seen in Figs. 9(h) and 10(h).

The melting behaviors of n-atom (
n = 6 , 10
) graphene quantum dots (GQD6 and zigzag GQD10) have been studied at different temperatures ranging from zero to 500K using finite-temperature CPMD simulations. The temperature dependence of the geometries of the quantum dots are thoroughly evaluated via calculating the related shape deformation parameters and the eigenvalues of the quadrupole tensors. The average values of the total energy and its different constituent terms including the kinetic energy of the nuclear and the fictitious electron dynamics, the exchange–correlation contribution, the electrostatic energy, the potential energy, and the local and non-local components of the pseudopotential energy as functions of temperature have been quantitatively calculated for each quantum dot. It is found that rise in temperature directly increases the fictitious electron dynamics as well as the total energy of each system in a way that the more the temperature, the less the stability of the atomic structure. Moreover, examining the variations of the root-mean-square bond length fluctuations and mean square displacements strongly proposes the value of
T
m
=
70K for the melting point of GQD6 while a continuous, two-stage melting process has been concluded for zigzag GQD10.
